question: I just moved into a new apartment in Boston and my landlord didn't put my security deposit into a separate account. Is that allowed?
expected_answer: No, in Massachusetts, landlords are required to hold a tenant's security deposit in a separate interest-bearing account and provide notice to the tenant about the account details. This is outlined in M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B.
key_facts: Landlords must hold security deposits in a separate interest-bearing account.
Landlords must provide notice to tenants about the security deposit account.
This requirement is specified in M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B.

source_chunks.title: Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits
Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know

question: My landlord stopped paying the electric bill, and I'm worried about losing service. What can I do according to Massachusetts law?
expected_answer: Under Massachusetts law, it is considered an unfair practice for a landlord who is required to provide gas or electric service to fail to pay the bills, exposing tenants to the risk of losing such services. You may have legal grounds to take action if this obligation is not met.
key_facts: It is an unfair practice for a landlord to fail to provide required gas or electric services.
The landlord must pay gas or electric bills when due to prevent service loss.
Tenants are protected from losing services due to landlord's failure to pay utility bills.

source_chunks.title: 940 CMR 3.17 — Landlord-Tenant Consumer Protection Regulations
940 CMR 3.17 — Landlord-Tenant Consumer Protection Regulations

question: Can my landlord charge me a pet fee in addition to my rent in Boston?
expected_answer: No, it is illegal for landlords in Boston to charge a pet fee. They can only charge for first and last month’s rent, a security deposit, and a lock fee.
key_facts: It is illegal for you to charge a holding fee, pet fee, or broker or finder’s fee.
You can legally charge a tenant for first and last month’s rent, security deposit, and lock fee.

question: I'm renting an apartment in Boston and my lease is for 12 months. What are my rights regarding the security deposit my landlord is holding?
expected_answer: As a tenant in Boston, your landlord must follow the rules in MGL c. 186, s.15B for handling your security deposit. This includes placing it in an interest-bearing account in a Massachusetts bank, providing you with the bank details within 30 days, and giving you a statement of condition. If the landlord fails to comply, you may be entitled to triple damages plus interest, court costs, and attorney's fees.
key_facts: MGL c. 186, s.15B outlines the requirements for handling security deposits.
Landlords must deposit the security deposit in an interest-bearing account in a Massachusetts bank and provide the tenant with the bank details within 30 days.
Failure to properly handle the deposit can result in triple damages for the tenant.

source_chunks.title: Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits
Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits

question: I just found out my landlord is selling the building. What happens to my security deposit?
expected_answer: When a landlord transfers their interest in a dwelling unit, they must transfer any security deposit held to the new owner. The new owner is then responsible for the deposit in accordance with M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B.
key_facts: When a landlord sells a building, they must transfer the security deposit to the new owner.
The new owner has 45 days to send the tenant notice about the security deposit.
This requirement is specified in M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B.

source_chunks.title: Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits
Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits

question: Can my landlord in Boston legally require me to pay for the cost of repairing normal wear and tear when I move out?
expected_answer: No, under Massachusetts law, a landlord cannot deduct the cost of repairing normal wear and tear from your security deposit. M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B specifies that deductions from a security deposit are limited to unpaid rent, unpaid increase in real estate taxes, and damages beyond normal wear and tear.
key_facts: A landlord cannot charge tenants for normal wear and tear repairs.
Deductions are only allowed for unpaid rent, unpaid increase in real estate taxes, and damages beyond normal wear and tear.
This is specified in M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B.

source_chunks.title: Massachusetts law about tenants' security deposits
Legal Tactics Ch. 3: Security Deposits

question: Can my landlord in Boston legally turn off my heat during the winter if I'm late on rent?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot legally turn off your heat if you are late on rent. Massachusetts law requires landlords to provide essential services like heat, and willfully failing to do so is a violation of MGL c.186 s.14. Such actions can result in fines or imprisonment for the landlord.
key_facts: Massachusetts law requires landlords to provide essential services like heat.
Willfully failing to provide heat is a violation of MGL c.186 s.14.
Violations can lead to fines or imprisonment for the landlord.

source_chunks.title: MGL c.186 s.14 - Quiet enjoyment / wrongful acts of landlord

question: Can my landlord in Boston evict me because I reported a building code violation to the city?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot evict you in retaliation for reporting a building code violation. Massachusetts law protects tenants from reprisals when they report violations of health or building codes to the appropriate authorities.
key_facts: MGL c.186 s.18 prohibits reprisals against tenants for reporting building code violations.
Retaliatory eviction is a common defense against eviction under Massachusetts law.
Landlords must follow a formal eviction process and cannot evict tenants for protected activities.

source_chunks.title: MGL c.186 s.18 - Anti-Retaliation Protection for Tenants

question: Can my landlord evict me because I reported a health violation to the city?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot evict you for reporting a health violation to the city, as this would be considered retaliatory eviction, which is prohibited. Massachusetts law protects tenants from reprisals for reporting violations of law. If the eviction notice is retaliatory, it can be challenged.
key_facts: Under MGL c.239 s.2A, it is a defense to eviction that the action was taken in reprisal for the tenant reporting code violations or exercising legal rights.
A landlord cannot evict a tenant by physically removing them or changing the locks; they must go through the court process.

question: I'm facing eviction in Boston and I have kids in public school. Are there any programs that can help us?
expected_answer: Yes, the Access to Counsel Program in Boston offers full legal representation to families at risk of eviction, specifically for households with children enrolled in K-12 schools.
key_facts: Access to Counsel Program provides legal representation for families with children in K-12 schools to prevent evictions.
The program is a partnership between the Mayor's Office of Housing, Boston Public Schools, Family Aid Boston, and Greater Boston Legal Services.
This service is available only in Boston.

question: What steps should I take to prepare for an eviction court hearing in Boston?
expected_answer: Before going to court for an eviction, it's important for tenants to gather all relevant documents, such as lease agreements, rent payment receipts, and any communication with the landlord. This preparation helps present a strong case. Tenants should also familiarize themselves with the eviction process as outlined in Chapter 239 of the Massachusetts General Laws.
key_facts: A landlord must file a case in court and obtain a summary process judgment to evict a tenant.
Tenants have the right to respond to an eviction case, present defenses and counterclaims, and bring documents and witnesses to trial.
If a tenant does not file an answer or appear, the court may enter a default judgment against them.

question: Can I withhold rent if my apartment in Boston has serious code violations?
expected_answer: Yes, tenants in Massachusetts, including Boston, can withhold rent if there are serious code violations affecting health and safety. According to MGL c.239 s.8A, tenants may withhold rent to cover the difference between the agreed rent and the fair value of the premises, and any expenses incurred to rectify the violations.
key_facts: Massachusetts law allows tenants to withhold rent when there are housing code violations that endanger health or safety.
Under MGL c.239 s.8A, a tenant can claim the difference between the agreed rent and the fair value of the premises with defects.
The landlord must have knowledge of the conditions and fail to take reasonable steps to correct them.

source_chunks.title: Attorney General's Guide to Landlord and Tenant Rights

question: Can I withhold rent in Boston if my landlord hasn't fixed the heating in my apartment?
expected_answer: Yes, you may withhold rent if your apartment has code violations like lack of heating, provided you meet certain conditions, such as notifying the landlord and allowing a reasonable time for repairs. The premises also must not be in a hotel, motel, or lodging house where you've stayed less than three months (MGL c.239 s.8A).
key_facts: Tenants must notify the landlord of housing code violations and give reasonable time to repair before withholding rent.
Rent withholding is permissible when conditions violate the sanitary code and impair health or safety.
The tenant should document the conditions and keep records of all communications with the landlord.

question: I just got an eviction notice in Boston. When do I need to appear in court, and what happens if I don't respond in time?
expected_answer: You need to appear in court on the date specified in the summons, and it's crucial to respond by this date to avoid a default judgment against you. Your response, including any answer or counterclaims, must be filed before the court date. If you fail to respond or appear, you may lose the case by default, which can lead to eviction.
key_facts: Tenants must file an answer with the court before the answer date on the summons.
Tenants can raise defenses and counterclaims in response to an eviction.
If a tenant fails to file an answer or appear, the court may enter a default judgment and order eviction.

question: I'm struggling to pay my rent in a Boston public housing unit. Is there any way I can get an exemption or reduction due to my financial situation?
expected_answer: Yes, you can apply for a hardship exemption if you're facing severe financial difficulties. The housing authority must consider exemptions for situations such as waiting for eligibility determination for assistance programs, involuntary job loss, or a death in the household. Refer to MGL c.121B s.32 for more details on the criteria for hardship exemptions.
key_facts: MGL c.121B s.32 outlines the conditions under which a tenant can be exempt from the minimum rent due to severe financial hardship.
The exemption can be applied if the tenant is awaiting eligibility for assistance programs or has experienced a significant change in income.
The housing authority is responsible for determining eligibility for the hardship exemption.

source_chunks.title: MGL c.121B s.32 - Public housing maintenance, operation, tenant selection and evictions

question: Can my landlord increase my rent during a market emergency in Boston?
expected_answer: Under Massachusetts law, a landlord cannot unfairly increase rent during a market emergency, as this is considered an unfair or deceptive act under 940 CMR 3.17.
key_facts: 940 CMR 3.17 makes it an unfair or deceptive practice for a landlord to rent a unit with conditions that violate law or endanger health and safety.
Landlords are prohibited from failing to maintain dwelling units in compliance with the sanitary code.
The regulation covers conditions at the inception of tenancy and ongoing maintenance obligations.

question: Can my landlord enter my apartment without notice to make repairs?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot enter your apartment without notice to make repairs unless there is an emergency. They should provide at least one day's notice and try to arrange a convenient time with you.
key_facts: The landlord should provide at least one day’s notice (24 hours) and attempt to arrange a convenient time to access the unit.
Unless there is an emergency, the landlord should never enter your apartment without advance notice and your permission.

question: My apartment in Boston has been unbearably hot this winter, constantly over 78°F. Is my landlord violating any regulations?
expected_answer: Yes, your landlord may be violating Massachusetts regulations. According to 105 CMR 410.180(B), the heating system during the heating season should not cause the temperature to exceed 78°F in any room.
key_facts: 105 CMR 410.180 states the heating system must maintain at least 68F between 7AM-11PM and 64F between 11PM-7AM during heating season.
The heating system shall not cause the temperature to exceed 78F in any room (105 CMR 410.180(B)).
Temperature is measured at a height of five feet above floor level.

question: Can my landlord in Boston be penalized if they don't fix a serious health code violation in my apartment after being ordered to?
expected_answer: Yes, if your landlord fails to comply with an order to correct a violation, they may face penalties. According to 105 CMR 410.920, a landlord who does not address the violation can be subject to legal action and fines.
key_facts: Under 105 CMR 410.920, any person who fails to comply with an order shall be fined not less than $10 nor more than $500 per violation.
Each day's failure to comply with an order constitutes a separate violation.
The board of health can take enforcement action including ordering corrections and assessing penalties.

question: Can my landlord block the hallway with furniture in our apartment building in Boston?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot block the hallway with furniture in a multi-unit residence. The owner is responsible for maintaining all common areas, including hallways, free from obstruction to ensure safe passage for all residents.
key_facts: Under 105 CMR 410.260, every dwelling unit shall have means of exit allowing safe passage in accordance with the building code.
No person shall cause obstruction of any means of egress or passageway.
The owner shall maintain free from obstruction every means of egress in common areas used by occupants of more than one dwelling unit.

question: My landlord mentioned my building might have asbestos. What are they required to do about it?
expected_answer: Your landlord must maintain all asbestos-containing materials in good repair, ensuring they are free from defects like holes or cracks that could release asbestos dust. If there is any violation, they must correct it in compliance with accepted standards.
key_facts: Under 105 CMR 410.250, the owner shall maintain all asbestos-containing materials in intact condition.
The sanitary code defines asbestos-containing material and requires owners to ensure it does not release fibers.
Occupants must maintain appliances and facilities in sanitary condition and report hazardous conditions to the owner.

question: My landlord in Boston is trying to keep my entire security deposit after I accidentally damaged a wall. Is this allowed?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot retain your entire security deposit unless the damages exceed the deposit amount. The landlord is only entitled to keep an amount that corresponds to actual damages incurred.
key_facts: A landlord can only deduct from a security deposit for damage beyond normal wear and tear.
The landlord must provide an itemized list of damages with cost estimates within 30 days after the tenancy ends.

question: Can my landlord charge me an exorbitant fee for emergency repairs during a declared state of emergency in Massachusetts?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot charge an unconscionably high fee for emergency repairs during a declared state of emergency. According to 940 CMR 3.17, a price is considered unconscionably high if there's a gross disparity between the charged price and the usual price before the emergency, or what other businesses charge, unless justified by increased costs.
key_facts: 940 CMR 3.18 prohibits charging unconscionably high prices during a declared state of emergency.
A price is unconscionable if there is a gross disparity with pre-emergency prices or other businesses' prices.
The disparity must not be substantially attributable to increased supplier prices or abnormal market disruptions.

source_chunks.title: 940 CMR 3.17 — Landlord-Tenant Consumer Protection Regulations

question: Can my landlord enter my apartment without my permission in Boston?
expected_answer: Under Massachusetts law, a landlord can only enter a tenant's apartment with proper notice and for specific reasons, such as to inspect the premises, make repairs, or show the apartment to prospective tenants. They must provide reasonable notice, except in emergencies.
key_facts: The landlord should provide at least one day's notice (24 hours) before entering a tenant's apartment.
A landlord may enter to inspect the premises, make repairs, or show the unit to prospective tenants or purchasers.
Unless there is an emergency, the landlord should never enter without advance notice and the tenant's permission.

question: Can my landlord in Boston charge me a late fee if I'm only a few days late on rent?
expected_answer: Under Massachusetts law, a landlord cannot charge a late fee until the rent is 30 days overdue. This regulation is outlined in 940 CMR 3.17(3)(a). Therefore, if you are only a few days late, your landlord cannot legally impose a late fee.
key_facts: A late fee can only be charged if there is a written agreement that allows it.
Even with a written agreement, the late fee cannot be charged unless rent is at least 30 days late.
There is no grace period for payment of rent; rent is due on the date specified in the tenancy terms.

source_chunks.title: Top Ten Things Tenants and Landlords Need to Know

question: Can my landlord shut off my electricity if I’m behind on rent payments in Boston?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot shut off your electricity because you are behind on rent payments. According to MGL c.186 s.14, a landlord is prohibited from willfully or intentionally interrupting utilities to a tenant as a means to enforce payment of rent. Such actions can result in penalties against the landlord.
key_facts: Under MGL c.186 s.14, a landlord who is required to furnish utilities may not willfully fail to furnish them.
A landlord who violates this section may be liable for actual and consequential damages or three months' rent, whichever is greater.
The tenant may also recover costs and attorney's fees for such violations.

source_chunks.title: MGL c.186 s.14 - Quiet enjoyment / wrongful acts of landlord

question: Can my landlord shut off my water if I am behind on rent?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot shut off your water even if you are behind on rent. This is considered a violation of your right to quiet enjoyment under Massachusetts law.
key_facts: Under MGL c.186 s.14, it is unlawful for a landlord to willfully shut off or fail to provide water service.
Interference with utilities constitutes a violation of the tenant's right to quiet enjoyment.
Tenants can seek damages including three months' rent or actual damages, whichever is greater.

question: Can my landlord shut off my water if I am behind on rent in Boston?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot shut off your water even if you are behind on rent. This is considered a violation of your right to quiet enjoyment, and you may be entitled to damages or other legal remedies.

question: Can my landlord in Boston deny me housing because of my religion?
expected_answer: No, under Massachusetts law, it is unlawful for a landlord to deny you housing because of your religious creed. This is prohibited under MGL Chapter 151B, which addresses unlawful discrimination in housing based on factors such as race, color, religious creed, national origin, ancestry, or sex.
key_facts: MGL c.151B s.4 prohibits refusing to rent or lease housing because of a person's religious creed.
Unlawful discrimination in housing includes factors such as race, color, religious creed, national origin, sex, and other protected classes.
Violations can be reported to the Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination (MCAD).

question: I heard Boston has some new rules about affordable housing requirements for developers. Can someone explain what these are?
expected_answer: Boston's updated Inclusionary Zoning requires market-rate housing developments with seven or more units to create 15 to 17 percent of income-restricted units on-site or nearby, plus an additional three percent for voucher holders in large rental projects. Developers can also contribute to a fund instead of creating these units. These changes are effective from October 2024.
key_facts: Boston's Inclusionary Zoning requires market-rate developments with seven or more units to create income-restricted housing at 15 to 17 percent of units.
Large rental projects must additionally provide 3 percent of units for voucher holders.
Developers may contribute to the Inclusionary Development Policy Fund in lieu of building income-restricted units on site.

question: I'm a renter in Boston and my landlord wants to switch my lease to a month-to-month agreement. What are the pros and cons of this change?
expected_answer: Switching to a month-to-month agreement, known as a tenancy-at-will, offers more flexibility as it runs from month to month. However, a lease provides more security typically running for one year, ensuring stability in your rental terms.
key_facts: A tenancy-at-will agreement offers more flexibility, but a lease offers more security.
You can offer a tenant a lease, which typically runs for one year, or a verbal or written tenancy-at-will agreement, which runs from month to month.

question: Can my landlord make me pay a broker fee if I'm renting an apartment in Boston?
expected_answer: No, your landlord cannot make you pay a broker fee for services provided to the landlord. As of August 1, 2025, brokers and salespeople are prohibited from charging fees to tenants when they primarily provide services to the landlord.
key_facts: A new law went into effect on August 1, 2025, prohibiting brokers from charging fees to tenants when they primarily provide services to the landlord.

question: I heard my apartment building is being foreclosed. I have a lease with a rental subsidy. What happens to my lease after foreclosure?
expected_answer: If your rental payment is subsidized under state or federal law, the foreclosure will not affect your tenancy agreement. The foreclosing entity must assume your lease and the rental subsidy contract.
key_facts: MGL c.186 s.13A states that foreclosure does not affect the tenancy agreement of a tenant whose rental payment is subsidized under state or federal law.
The foreclosing entity must assume the lease and rental subsidy contract.
Your tenancy will be considered as a tenant at will upon foreclosure.

question: Can I ask for a modification to my lease terms due to my disability if I'm struggling to meet certain requirements?

This means the BHA can consider your disability and work with you to find an arrangement that allows you to comply with the program.

question: I'm a single applicant looking for housing in Boston. Can I choose between a studio and a 1-bedroom unit?
expected_answer: As a new admissions single individual, you will be assigned to either a studio or a 1-bedroom unit based on availability, unless you provide documentation stating the necessity for a 1-bedroom. The BHA plans to allow single applicants to choose to be on a waiting list for just one unit type in the future.
key_facts: New admissions single individuals are assigned to whichever unit size, studio or 1-bedroom, is first available.

expected_answer: Yes, the BHA can offer a different accommodation if it's less burdensome and equally effective in meeting your needs.

question: I'm on the waiting list for a BHA apartment in Boston. Can I change the development choices I've selected?
expected_answer: Yes, you can add or remove development choices at any time before the final eligibility screening process. However, once you are contacted by the BHA to schedule the personal interview appointment, no changes in development choice(s) will be accepted.
key_facts: Applicants may add or remove developments choices at any time prior to entering the final eligibility screening process.
Once the applicant is contacted by the BHA to schedule the personal interview appointment no changes in development choice(s) shall be accepted.
Applicants are encouraged to visit the public housing developments before applying or before entering the final screening process.

question: Is my landlord required to provide a refrigerator in my Boston apartment?
expected_answer: No, landlords in Boston are not required to provide refrigerators in rental units. However, if they do provide one, they must maintain it in working condition.
key_facts: Landlords are not required to provide refrigerators, but if they offer them, they must maintain them.
Under 105 CMR 410, a refrigerator with freezer is only required if specified in a written rental agreement.

question: Do I need to have renters insurance as a tenant in Boston?
expected_answer: While renters insurance is not legally required for tenants in Boston, it is recommended as a small price to pay in case of a big problem.
key_facts: Renters insurance is not legally required in Massachusetts.
The City of Boston recommends renters insurance to cover damages or losses from fire or other disasters.
The landlord is generally not responsible for fire damage to tenant's belongings absent negligence.

question: Do I need to get renters insurance for my apartment in Boston?
expected_answer: While it is not legally required to have renters insurance in Boston, it is highly recommended as a small price to pay for protection against potential big problems.
key_facts: Renters insurance is not legally required by Massachusetts law.
Renters insurance can cover damage or loss to personal belongings from events like fire.
Without insurance, tenants bear the cost of replacing belongings damaged by fire or disaster.

question: My landlord never put my $2,000 security deposit in a separate bank account, and now that I've moved out 45 days ago, they still haven't returned it. What legal steps can I take and what damages can I recover?
expected_answer: In Massachusetts, your landlord has violated multiple requirements of the security deposit law (M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B). They were required to place your deposit in a separate interest-bearing bank account and return it within 30 days after your tenancy ended. You should first send a demand letter via certified mail. If they don't respond, you can take them to Small Claims court (up to $7,000 limit, though statutory damages may exceed this). The court must award you 3 times the security deposit amount for failure to properly deposit or return it, plus interest, court costs, and attorney's fees.
key_facts: Landlord must place security deposit in a separate interest-bearing bank account protected from creditors.
Landlord must return security deposit within 30 days after tenancy ends.
Tenant should send a demand letter first via certified mail, return receipt requested.
If landlord fails to properly deposit or return, court must award 3 times the security deposit amount.
Tenant can also recover interest, court costs, and attorney's fees.
Can file in Small Claims court for up to $7,000; 3x statutory damages may exceed $7,000.

question: I have a 4-year-old child and discovered peeling paint in our pre-1978 apartment. I reported it to my landlord and the Board of Health. Now my landlord served me with an eviction notice. Is this legal, and what can I do?
expected_answer: This eviction is almost certainly illegal on two grounds. First, under the Massachusetts Lead Law, your landlord is required to remove or cover all lead paint hazards in pre-1978 homes where a child under 6 lives, and cannot refuse to rent to or evict you because you have children or because of lead paint. Second, under M.G.L. c. 186 § 18, it is illegal for a landlord to retaliate against a tenant for reporting violations to the Board of Health. You can raise retaliation as both a defense and a counterclaim in the eviction proceedings. If the court finds retaliation, the landlord may be ordered to pay you 1-3 months' rent in damages.
key_facts: Massachusetts Lead Law requires landlords to remove or cover lead paint hazards in pre-1978 homes where a child under 6 lives.
Landlord cannot refuse to rent to or evict a tenant because they have children or because of lead paint.
It is illegal for a landlord to retaliate for reporting a lead paint violation to the Board of Health under M.G.L. c. 186 § 18.
Tenant can raise retaliation as both a defense and counterclaim in eviction proceedings.
If court finds retaliation, landlord may be ordered to pay 1-3 months' rent in damages.

question: My apartment has had no heat since December even though my landlord is responsible for it, and I've told them multiple times in writing. I started withholding rent and now they're trying to evict me for nonpayment. What are my legal rights?
expected_answer: You have strong legal protections. First, your landlord is violating the State Sanitary Code, which requires minimum heat of 68°F from 7am-11pm and 64°F at other times during heating season (September 15 - June 15). This failure to maintain habitable conditions after notice is also an unfair/deceptive practice under 940 CMR 3.17. You have the right to withhold rent for serious code violations, but should put the withheld money in a separate bank account. Bad conditions are a defense to a nonpayment eviction. A judge can determine the fair rental value of your apartment in its defective condition and calculate how much rent you actually owe. You may also have a counterclaim for damages based on the decreased value of the apartment.
key_facts: Landlord must maintain minimum heat of 68°F (7am-11pm) and 64°F (11pm-7am) during heating season (Sept 15 - June 15).
Failure to maintain habitable conditions after notice is an unfair/deceptive practice under 940 CMR 3.17.
Tenant may withhold rent for serious code violations but should put withheld rent in a separate bank account.
Bad conditions are a defense to nonpayment eviction if conditions violate Sanitary Code and landlord had knowledge.
A judge can determine fair rental value of the apartment in its defective condition and calculate how much rent is actually owed.
Tenant may also have a counterclaim for damages based on the decreased value of the apartment.

question: My landlord is responsible for gas service per our lease, but they stopped paying the bill. The gas company sent a shutoff notice. What are my rights, and can I deduct utility payments from my rent?
expected_answer: Under 940 CMR 3.17, it is an unfair practice for a landlord who is obligated to provide gas service to fail to pay the bills and expose tenants to loss of service. The utility company must give tenants at least 30 days' notice before shutting off service when the landlord fails to pay. You and your neighbors can band together to pay a 'projected bill' to avoid the shutoff, and you have a right to deduct those payments from your rent. You are protected from retaliation for withholding rent for this purpose. Additionally, failure to provide utilities violates your right to quiet enjoyment, and you can sue the landlord for up to 3 months' rent and attorney's fees.
key_facts: Under 940 CMR 3.17, it is an unfair practice for a landlord obligated to provide gas/electric to fail to provide service or expose tenants to risk of loss by failing to pay bills.
Utility company must give tenants at least 30 days' notice before shutting off service when landlord fails to pay.
Tenants can band together to pay a projected bill to avoid shutoff and deduct those payments from rent.
Tenants are protected from retaliation for withholding rent for utility payments.
Failure to provide utilities violates quiet enjoyment — tenants can win up to 3 months' rent and attorney's fees.

question: I came home and found my landlord changed the locks on my apartment. My belongings are still inside. What are my legal options and what damages can I recover?
expected_answer: What your landlord did is an illegal lockout. In Massachusetts, a landlord cannot lock out a tenant or remove their belongings without a court order. Under M.G.L. c. 186 § 15F, if a tenant is removed from or excluded from their premises without a valid court order, they can recover possession or terminate the rental agreement, and in either case recover three months' rent or three times actual damages, plus the cost of suit including reasonable attorney's fees. A lockout also violates your right to quiet enjoyment and can be raised as a defense and counterclaim in any subsequent eviction. Additionally, a willful violation of M.G.L. c. 186 § 14 constitutes an unfair practice under 940 CMR 3.17 consumer protection regulations.
key_facts: A landlord cannot lock out a tenant or remove belongings without a court order.
Under M.G.L. c. 186 § 15F, tenant can recover 3 months' rent or 3 times actual damages, plus attorney's fees.
Any lease provision waiving the tenant's right to claim constructive eviction is void as against public policy.
Lockout is a violation of quiet enjoyment and can be raised as a defense/counterclaim in eviction.
Willful violation of M.G.L. c. 186 § 14 is an unfair practice under 940 CMR 3.17.

question: I'm being evicted for nonpayment of rent. My landlord never gave me a statement of condition when I moved in, never paid me interest on my security deposit, and I never got a receipt. Can I use any of this in court to fight the eviction?
expected_answer: Yes, security deposit law violations give you both a defense and a counterclaim in a nonpayment eviction. You have a defense to eviction if your landlord failed to provide a written receipt, give you a statement of condition, hold the deposit in a separate bank account, or pay you yearly interest. As a counterclaim, you may be entitled to three times the security deposit amount and interest owed. If you win the security deposit counterclaim but still owe rent, the court will give you 7 days to pay the difference to keep your apartment. The landlord also cannot claim you caused damage to the apartment if they didn't provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days.
key_facts: If landlord failed to provide receipt, statement of condition, separate bank account, or yearly interest, tenant has a defense to eviction.
Security deposit violations can be raised as both a defense and a counterclaim in nonpayment evictions.
As a counterclaim, tenant may be entitled to 3 times the security deposit and interest owed.
If tenant wins counterclaim but still owes rent, court gives 7 days to pay the difference to keep the apartment.
Landlord cannot claim tenant caused damage if they didn't provide an itemized list within 30 days.

question: My building was just foreclosed on by the bank. The new owner says I need to leave within 30 days. I've lived here for 5 years with a lease. What are my rights?
expected_answer: Under M.G.L. c. 186A, a foreclosing owner can only evict a tenant with 'just cause,' which is limited to: failure to pay rent, material lease violation not cured within 30 days, nuisance/substantial damage, illegal use, refusal to execute a renewal, or refusal of reasonable access. A bona fide lease must be honored and you cannot be evicted simply because of the foreclosure. Even if eviction proceedings begin, you can request a stay of eviction for up to 6 months (or 12 months if you are elderly or disabled) if the tenancy was terminated without your fault. To get a stay, you must show you cannot find suitable alternative housing and have made reasonable efforts to do so.
key_facts: Under M.G.L. c. 186A, a foreclosing owner can only evict a tenant with just cause (nonpayment, material violation, nuisance, illegal activity, refusal of access).
A bona fide lease must be honored — tenant cannot be evicted simply because of foreclosure.
Tenant can request a stay of eviction for up to 6 months (or 12 months if elderly/disabled) if tenancy terminated without fault.
To get a stay, tenant must show they cannot find suitable alternative housing and have made reasonable effort.

question: My landlord told me he doesn't want to rent to families with children and is now trying to evict me after I had a baby. Where can I file a complaint and can I fight the eviction?
expected_answer: Your landlord's actions are illegal housing discrimination. In Boston, it is illegal to discriminate in housing based on family status, among other protected classes. You can file a complaint with the Boston Fair Housing and Equity office by calling 617-635-2500 or completing their online intake form. In the eviction proceedings, discrimination is both a defense and a counterclaim that you can raise in nonpayment, fault, and no-fault evictions. Refusing to rent to or evicting someone because they have children violates both state and federal fair housing law.
key_facts: In Boston, it is illegal to discriminate in housing based on family status.
Tenant can file a complaint with Boston Fair Housing and Equity at 617-635-2500 or via online intake form.
Discrimination is a defense and counterclaim in nonpayment, fault, and no-fault eviction proceedings.
Refusing to rent to or evicting someone because they have children is illegal under state and federal fair housing law.

question: My landlord has refused to fix a leaking roof and mold problem for 6 months despite my written requests. I've called the Board of Health and they issued a repair order, but my landlord still hasn't fixed it. What court actions can I take?
expected_answer: You have several court options. First, you can file a Tenant Petition asking a judge to order your landlord to repair the Sanitary Code violations. A judge can order repairs, appoint a receiver (a temporary landlord) to make repairs, or lower your rent to the fair rental value of the apartment in its defective condition. Second, you can file a civil complaint seeking money damages for the harm you've suffered. Third, your landlord's failure to repair after notice is an unfair/deceptive practice under 940 CMR 3.17, which triggers consumer protection remedies under M.G.L. c. 93A — you can recover double or treble damages plus attorney's fees.
key_facts: Tenant can file a Tenant Petition asking a judge to order landlord to repair Sanitary Code violations.
A judge can order repairs, appoint a receiver (temporary landlord) to make repairs, or lower rent to fair rental value.
Tenant can file a civil complaint seeking money damages for harm suffered from bad conditions.
Failure to repair after notice is an unfair/deceptive practice under 940 CMR 3.17.
Under M.G.L. c. 93A, tenant can recover double or treble damages plus attorney's fees for unfair practices.

question: My landlord just told me he's converting our building to condominiums and is raising my rent by 50%. He also said the new condo owner won't be responsible for my security deposit. Is any of this legal?
expected_answer: No, none of this is legal. Under Massachusetts condo conversion law, a landlord or condo owner cannot increase your rent by more than 10% per year or above the increase in the Consumer Price Index, whichever is less. The owner only needs to have an 'intent to convert' for these tenant protections to apply. If you have a lease, the landlord cannot change your rent until the lease ends, except through a tax escalator clause. Regarding your security deposit, when a building is sold or ownership changes, the new owner becomes responsible for your last month's rent and security deposit, even if the former landlord did not transfer the deposit. If the new owner fails to honor this, you can take them to court for up to 3 times the security deposit amount.
key_facts: Under condo conversion law, rent cannot increase by more than 10% per year or above the CPI increase, whichever is less.
If tenant has a lease, landlord cannot change rent until the lease ends (except through a tax escalator clause).
When ownership changes, the new owner becomes responsible for last month's rent and security deposit paid to the former landlord.
This is true even if the former landlord did not transfer the deposit to the new owner.
The owner must have only an 'intent to convert' for tenant protections to apply.
